era_context: Han Dynasty under Emperor Wu is expanding aggressively. The Xiongnu confederation remains powerful but increasingly pressured. Rome is in the late Republic, with the Gracchian reform movement gaining momentum under Gaius Gracchus. The Mediterranean is dominated by Rome, Egypt, and Seleucid fragments. Parthia is rising in the Near East. This is a period of significant geopolitical realignment across Eurasia.

events.title: Gaius Gracchus elected tribune of the plebs; beginning of radical reform program
events.description: Gaius Gracchus was elected tribune of the plebs in 123 BCE, following in the footsteps of his brother Tiberius (killed 133 BCE). He immediately began implementing a radical reform program that included land redistribution, grain subsidies for the poor, and extension of political rights to non-landowners. His reforms were popular with the masses but generated fierce opposition from the conservative Senate faction. Gracchus's election marked the beginning of a new phase of factional conflict in the Roman Republic that would destabilize it for decades.

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (compiled 1st century BCE–CE, fragmentary)

events.certainty_note: Gaius Gracchus's election as tribune in 123 BCE is well-attested in multiple sources. The year is secure.
cross_references: Follows from Tiberius Gracchus's reforms (133 BCE)
Precursor to Gaius Gracchus's death (121 BCE)

events.title: Han Dynasty military campaigns against the Xiongnu; consolidation of frontier
events.description: Emperor Wu of Han continued aggressive military campaigns against the Xiongnu confederation during the 120s BCE. Around 123 BCE, Han forces were consolidating control over frontier regions and establishing military colonies. These campaigns were part of a broader strategy to weaken the Xiongnu and secure trade routes. The exact chronology of individual campaigns is uncertain, but this period saw significant Han military activity and expansion.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: Han records document campaigns during the 120s BCE, but precise yearly attribution is uncertain (±2–3 years).
cross_references: Part of broader Han-Xiongnu conflict (c. 133–119 BCE)
Precursor to major Han victories in 119 BCE

events.title: Seleucid Empire fragmentation; loss of eastern territories to Parthia
events.description: The Seleucid Empire, once the dominant power in the Near East, was fragmenting by 123 BCE. Parthia was consolidating control over eastern territories, including Mesopotamia and the Iranian plateau. The Seleucids retained control of Syria and parts of Anatolia but were losing power and prestige. This period marks the transition from Seleucid to Parthian dominance in the Near East, a shift that would reshape the geopolitical balance of Eurasia.
key_figures: Antiochus VII Sidetes (Seleucid king, d. 129 BCE)
Seleucus VI (Seleucid king, r. c. 125–95 BCE)
Mithridates II of Parthia (r. c. 124–91 BCE)
events.certainty_note: The general process of Seleucid fragmentation is well-attested; specific events in 123 BCE are less precisely documented. Seleucid chronology is complex and debated.
cross_references: Precursor to Roman conquest of Syria (64 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Gaius Gracchus as a heroic reformer is contested by some modern scholars. Some sources date his election as tribune to 124 BCE rather than 123 BCE. Han military campaigns are often presented as a linear progression of victories, but recent scholarship suggests the conflict was more cyclical. The 'decline' of the Seleucid Empire is a modern historiographic construct; contemporaries may not have perceived it as such.
historiographic_note: 123 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Mediterranean world but less so for Asia. Roman sources are abundant but written long after events. Han Dynasty records are rich but compiled centuries after events and organized by reign rather than precise year. Seleucid history relies on numismatic evidence and later Greek/Roman accounts. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are absent from written records.

graph_edges.note: Gaius Gracchus's election and reforms in 123 BCE led to his violent death in 121 BCE
Han military campaigns of 123 BCE escalated into major victories in 119 BCE
Seleucid fragmentation of this period led to Roman conquest of Syria in 64 BCE
